content.text: Answer FOUR questions in detail. State carefully any results used without proof.

problem.text: Let \((a_n)_{n=0}^{\infty}\) be a bounded real sequence. Write \(L\) for the set of all real numbers \(\ell\) such that \((\exists N)(\forall n>N)\;\ell<a_n\) and write \(U\) for the set of all real numbers \(u\) such that \((\exists N)(\forall n>N)\;a_n<u\).

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: Give an example of a convergent sequence for which the inequality in (i) is strict, or prove that no such sequence exists.

problem.text: Let \(U\) be a subset of the metric space \(X\). Does either of the following conditions imply the other? Prove or disprove, as appropriate.

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: For any \(A\subseteq X\), if \(U\) meets the closure of \(A\) then \(U\) meets \(A\) itself.

problem.text: Let \(X\) and \(Y\) be metric spaces. Decide whether the following statements are true or false.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: If \((x_n)_{n=0}^{\infty}\) converges to \(x\) in \(X\) then \(\{x\}\cup\{x_n:n\geq 0\}\) is compact.

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: The function \(f:X\to Y\) is continuous if its restriction \(f|_K\) to each compact subset \(K\subseteq X\) is continuous.

problem.text: Let \(f:X\to Y\) be a continuous bijection with inverse \(g:Y\to X\) and assume that \(X\) is complete.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text: Show by example that if \(g\) is continuous then \(Y\) need not be complete.

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: Prove that if \(g\) is uniformly continuous then \(Y\) must be complete.

subparts.label: (i)
subparts.text:
When \(a\) is a positive real number, calculate
\[
\lim_{x\to\infty}\frac{f(x+a)-f(x)}{a}.
\]

subparts.label: (ii)
subparts.text: Assume further that \(f(x)\to A\in\mathbb{R}\) as \(x\to\infty\) and calculate \(L\).
